items.title: People overlook subtractive solutions to mental health problems
items.author: Nadia Adelina
items.content: Eight experimental and naturalistic studies show that people (and chatbots) tend to give advice to improve mental health that involve us doing more (e.g., take up yoga) and they neglect solutions that involve doing less (e.g., quit junk food).

items.title: Auditory-motor adaptation and de-adaptation for speech depend more on time in the new environment than on the amount of practice
items.author: Ludo Max
items.content: Speech auditory-motor adaptation to a formant-shift perturbation and de-adaptation after the perturbation is removed both depend more on total amount of time spent in the corresponding environment than on the number of practice trials.

items.title: Neural correlates reveal separate stages of spontaneous face perception
items.author: Jessica Taubert
items.content: Neural dynamics reveal separate stages of spontaneous face perception: EEG shows illusory faces in objects initially resemble real faces then shift to object-like representations, with task demands determining which identity guides behavior.

items.title: Social inequity disrupts reward-based learning
items.author: Adrianna C. Jenkins
items.content: People learn from rewards differently when outcomes are shared with others. Learning slows when receiving smaller reward shares, and social stereotypes about partners further impair learning when cognitive demands are low, showing social context shapes basic learning.

items.title: Identity fusion can foster intergroup trust and willingness to cooperate
items.author: Harvey Whitehouse
items.content: Identity fusion has traditionally been associated with intergroup violence. However, across two studies we show that fusion can predict increased willingness to trust and cooperate with outgroups, if contextual perceptions are positive.

items.title: Awe is characterized as an ambivalent affect in the human behavior and cortex
items.author: Jiook Cha
items.content: Using VR, EEG, and contrastive learning, this study found that subjective awe is better predicted by behavioral and neural representations of mixed feelings than by those of purely positive or negative ones, challenging a univalent viewpoint of awe.

items.title: Anticipatory eye gaze as a marker of memory
items.author: Yuval Nir
items.content: Anticipatory eye movements during repeated movie viewing reveal when and what is remembered. Gaze patterns correlate with explicit reports, offering a method to detect memory for events without verbal reports.

items.title: Veteran and first responder family members show distinct mental health networks centered on negative emotions
items.author: Oleg N. Medvedev
items.content: Network analysis of 317 family members of veterans and first responders reveals six distinct mental health symptom communities, with strong negative emotions and uncontrollable worry as central modes of psychological distress.
